name: Brahmadatta
dates_notes: Cited by Sureśvara and Vāchaspati; no surviving work; positions reconstructed from later citations.
core_thesis: As reconstructed from later citations: Brahmadatta is a pre-Śaṅkara Vedāntin known almost entirely through hostile citation in Śaṅkara's school — Sureśvara in the *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi*, Maṇḍana Miśra in the *Brahma-Siddhi*, and (less directly) Śaṅkara himself. The position firmly attributed to him is *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: liberation requires the conjoined cultivation of upaniṣadic *jñāna* and prescribed Vedic *karma*; ritual action is not subordinated to or dropped at the moment of knowledge but remains constitutive. Coupled with this is a *bhāvanā* thesis: knowledge of Brahman, like ritual injunction, becomes liberating only through repeated meditative cultivation (*prasaṃkhyāna* / *bhāvanā*) — direct, single-event *anubhava* is not sufficient. Sureśvara writes against him precisely on this point, defending the immediacy of knowledge against the *bhāvanā*-requirement. Brahmadatta thus stands in the tradition as the named alternative — Mīmāṃsā-respectful, ritually-continuous Vedānta — that the Śaṅkara-school had to reject in establishing *jñāna* alone as the direct cause of *mokṣa*.

engaged_works.title: Lost commentary on Upaniṣads
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Work itself is lost; bhāvanā-thesis preserved through citations in Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi and Vāchaspati's writings.
engaged_works.summary: Brahmadatta's commentary survives only through the polemical citations that the Śaṅkara school directs against it. Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (especially chapters 1 and 3) names Brahmadatta as the proponent of two linked theses: (i) *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* — that knowledge and ritual action together constitute the means of *mokṣa*, neither sufficient alone; and (ii) *bhāvanā* / *prasaṃkhyāna* — that the direct knowledge generated by *mahāvākya*-hearing requires repeated meditative reinforcement before it becomes liberating. Maṇḍana's *Brahma-Siddhi* preserves a related *prasaṃkhyāna*-position that later commentators (Vāchaspati, Ānandagiri) explicitly link to Brahmadatta. Nothing of his actual commentary on the Upaniṣads survives; even the form of the work — whether *bhāṣya*, *vārttika*, or *prakaraṇa* — is uncertain. The historical Brahmadatta is recoverable only as the named opponent across whom Śaṅkara's school defines its own immediacy-of-knowledge thesis.
